titleInfo.title: Avalokiteśvara in Early Chinese Buddhist Translations: A Preliminary Survey

mods.abstract: 185: “...the now-standard name Guanshiyin 觀世音(often abbreviated Guanyin 觀音) came into use only around the beginning of the fifth century CE.” The earliest names for the bodhisattva are in translation Kuiyin 窺) 音 in the Fa jing jing 法鏡經 Ugraparipṛcchā-(sūtra by An Xuan 安玄 and Yan Fotiao 嚴佛調 and in transcription Elouxuan 廅樓亘 in T#361, which probably goes back to the form *Avalokasvara . In early Chinese translations, Avalokasvara appears only as a member of the audience listening to the Buddha preaching, and plays an extremely minimal role. It becomes only an object of devotion in the two earliest translations of the larger Sukhāvatīvyūha, that by Lokakṣema (T #361) and by Zhi Qian (T#362). 198: Both translations “recommend that ordinary pratitioners who find themselves in difficulty should turn to them [i.e. Avalokasvara and Mahāsthāmaprāpta] for aid” In a second appearance of Avalokasvara in Zhi Qian’s translation, he receives his prediction (vyākarana) of his futur buddhahood from Buddha Amitābha. 200-201: Nattier translates T#361, 12:288b19-24 202: Avalokasvara as the successor of Amitābha Buddha “The most dramatic passage concerning Avalokasvara, however, occurs in a prose portion of the sūtra and is found in th etranslations of both Lokakṣema and Zhi Qian. For it is here that we are told that, after the death of Amitābha Buddha, Avalokasvara will succeed him as the reigning Buddha of Sukhāvati.” “With the exception of the name of the Buddha Amitābha—which is transcribed as Amituo 阿彌陀 throughout Lokakṣema’s version, but translated as Wuliang qingjing 無量清淨 “Measureless Purity” by Zhi Qian—the wording of the two versions is virtually identical...” 203: Nattier translates T#362, 12:309a14-24 2What we have here, in brief, is an account of the succession of one Buddha by another that is traditional in a certain sense, in that it adheres to the principle that there can only be one Buddha in any given world-system at a time. Thus Avalokasvara must remain a bodhisattva until after the death of Amitābha Buddha; only then can he [sic!] attain buddhahood himself.” “What is far from traditional, however, is the speed with which this takes place: Avalokasvara immediately attains buddhahood after the death of 204. Amitābha...” “...in Sukhāvatī the succession to the next Buddha is immediate. Amitābha’s land is thus presented as a “perpetual buddha-field” in the sense that the Dharma will never die out.” BUT: Other version of the Larger Sukhāvatīvyūha in Sanskrit, Tibetan and later Chinese versions, have “no reference to Amitābha’s final nirvāna; only the mentions of his immeasurably long lifespan...” This scenario “is profoundly continuous with what we find in another early Pure Land text, the Akṣobhyavyūha, which is also considered to have been translated by Lokakṣema or a member of his school(Achu foguo jing 阿閦佛國經, T313). In this scripture ... much attention is paid to Akṣobhya’s dramatic final nirvāna, and it is also clear that no other bodhisattva can attain buddhahood in this world after his death. Taken together, these scriptures suggest that an important turning point in the evolution of Pure Land thought took place when all references to the eventual extinction of Amitābha were eliminated, and only the discussion of his immeasurable lifespan remained. “ 205: “In the larger Sukhāvatīvyūha we are told that he [Avalokasvara] can serve as a refuge for people in this world who are in need of help, that he has been predicted to buddhahood (at least in Zhi Qian’s version), and that he will succeed to the position of presiding Buddha of Sukhāvatī after Amitābha’s death.” Conclusions: “...the inclusion of the larger Sukhāvatīvyūha within the corpus of Han-period translations...” “... Avalokasvara’s first significant appearance ... is in the larger Sukhāvatīvyūha-sūtra, translated during the late second century CE (if by Lokakṣema himself) or perhaps slightly later (if by another member of his school). This version was available in the first half of the third century to Zhi Qian, who used it .... as the basis for his own translation.” 206: “Nonetheless it seems clear that it was through the work of Dharmaraksa—specifically, through the twenty-third chapter of his Lotus Sūtra, the Guangshiyin wumen pin 光世音五門品—that this bodhisattva first gained widespread appeal in China. For though we have found small traces of the veneration of this bodhisattva under the name Elouxuan, it was as Guangshiyin that he first became to be widely worshipped in China.”
